_meta.purpose: Curated SSOT for /endometriosis/ topic hub. Surgical comparison cards and ASRM stage reference rendered as Astro card grids.
_meta.source: Library citations, ASRM 2021 revised classification, Yeung et al. 2025, McDonnell et al. 2025.
_meta.card_notes: Each card field is an HTML fragment with inline citation hyperlinks. Render with Astro set:html. Inline citations are MANDATORY per editorial policy.

surgical_comparison.name: Excision
surgical_comparison.subtitle: Root-cause removal
surgical_comparison.description_html: The surgeon cuts out the endometriosis lesion and the underlying tissue into which it has grown. Nothing is left behind. Pathology specimen is preserved.
surgical_comparison.repeat_surgery_rate_html: 2.5% over multi-year follow-up in a prospective cohort of 620 patients. Yeung et al. 2025.
surgical_comparison.pathology_confirmation: Yes -- tissue sent for histologic confirmation
surgical_comparison.hormonal_suppression_post_op: Not necessary for good outcomes in complete excision, per Yeung et al. 2011.
surgical_comparison.rrm_stance: Endorsed as the only surgical standard

surgical_comparison.name: Ablation
surgical_comparison.subtitle: Surface destruction
surgical_comparison.description_html: Heat, laser, or electrical energy destroys the visible surface of a lesion. Disease that has grown beneath the surface is untouched. The specimen is destroyed.
surgical_comparison.repeat_surgery_rate_html: Up to 40 to 60% in historical series, per Yeung et al. 2025.
surgical_comparison.pathology_confirmation: No -- tissue destroyed by the procedure
surgical_comparison.hormonal_suppression_post_op: Frequently prescribed, but does not prevent disease progression
surgical_comparison.rrm_stance: Not endorsed. Inferior to excision on repeat-surgery outcomes.

asrm_stages.name: Stage I
asrm_stages.subtitle: Minimal
asrm_stages.features_html: Isolated implants, no significant adhesions. Surface lesions only.
asrm_stages.pain_correlation: No reliable correlation. Minimal-stage disease can cause severe pain.
asrm_stages.fertility_correlation: May contribute to infertility through inflammatory milieu even at low anatomic burden.
asrm_stages.clinical_note_html: Stage does not predict severity of patient experience. Take symptoms seriously regardless of staging.

asrm_stages.name: Stage II
asrm_stages.subtitle: Mild
asrm_stages.features_html: Superficial and/or deep implants, few adhesions.
asrm_stages.clinical_note_html: Mild staging does not mean mild disease burden for the individual patient.

asrm_stages.name: Stage III
asrm_stages.subtitle: Moderate
asrm_stages.features_html: Multiple deep implants, possible endometriomas, filmy adhesions.
asrm_stages.pain_correlation: More likely significant pain, including dyspareunia.
asrm_stages.fertility_correlation: Meaningful anatomic contribution to infertility begins. Surgical correction often indicated.
asrm_stages.clinical_note_html: Ovarian endometriomas and early adhesion formation typically appear at this stage.

asrm_stages.name: Stage IV
asrm_stages.subtitle: Severe
asrm_stages.features_html: Large endometriomas, dense adhesions, extensive deep infiltrating disease. May involve bowel, bladder, ureter.
asrm_stages.pain_correlation: Often significant, though some patients present primarily with infertility.
asrm_stages.fertility_correlation: Substantial anatomic distortion. Surgeon skill is paramount.
asrm_stages.clinical_note_html: Stage IV requires a surgeon experienced in complex excision, adhesiolysis, and potentially bowel or bladder surgery.
